content:
# Revelation Chapter 18 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 290-291 | **Source Image:** REV_018_page_001.jpg, REV_018_page_002.jpg

1. After these [things] I saw another angel coming down from the heaven, having great authority, and the earth was illuminated from the glory of him.

2. And he cried out in a strong voice, saying, Fallen, fallen [is] Babylon the great, and she became a dwelling of demons and a prison of every unclean spirit and a prison of every unclean and hated bird,

3. because from the wine of the wrath of the fornication of her all the nations have drunk, and the kings of the earth committed fornication with her, and the merchants of the earth from the power of the luxury of her became rich.

4. And I heard another voice from the heaven, saying, Come out from her, the people of me, that you may not share in the sins of her, and that from the plagues of her you may not receive,

5. because the sins of her were joined together unto the heaven, and the God remembered the unrighteous [deeds] of her.

6. Give back to her as also she gave back, and double the double [things] according to the works of her; in the cup which she mixed, mix to her double.

7. As much as she glorified herself and lived in luxury, so much torment and mourning give to her, because in the heart of her she says, I sit a queen, and a widow I am not, and mourning I will never see.

8. Because of this, in one day the plagues of her will come — death and mourning and famine — and in fire she will be burned, because strong [is the] Lord the God, the [one] having judged her.

9. And the kings of the earth, the [ones] having committed fornication with her and having lived in luxury, will weep and will mourn over her, when they see the smoke of the burning of her,

10. from far having stood on account of the fear of the torment of her, saying, Woe, woe, the great city, Babylon the strong city, because in one hour the judgment of you came.

11. And the merchants of the earth weep and mourn over her, because the cargo of them no one buys any more —

12. cargo of gold and of silver and of precious stone and of pearls and of fine linen and of purple and of silk and of scarlet, and every citron wood and every vessel of ivory and every vessel of most precious wood and of bronze and of iron and of marble,

13. and cinnamon and spice and incense and myrrh and frankincense and wine and oil and fine flour and wheat and cattle and sheep, and of horses and of chariots and of bodies and souls of men.

14. And the fruit of the desire of the soul of you departed from you, and all the fat [things] and the splendid [things] perished from you, and no longer in no [way] will they find them.

15. The merchants of these [things], the [ones] having become rich from her, from far will stand on account of the fear of the torment of her, weeping and mourning,

16. saying, Woe, woe, the great city, the [one] having been clothed in fine linen and purple and scarlet, and having been gilded in gold and precious stone and pearl,

17. because in one hour so great wealth was made desolate. And every shipmaster and everyone sailing upon a place, and sailors and as many as work the sea, from far stood,

18. and were crying out, seeing the smoke of the burning of her, saying, What [city is] like the great city?

19. And they cast dust upon the heads of them and were crying out, weeping and mourning, saying, Woe, woe, the great city, in which all the [ones] having the ships in the sea became rich from the costliness of her, because in one hour she was made desolate.

20. Rejoice over her, heaven, and the holy [ones] and the apostles and the prophets, because the God judged the judgment of you from her.

21. And one strong angel took up a stone like a great millstone, and cast [it] into the sea, saying, Thus with violence Babylon the great city will be thrown down, and will not be found any more.

22. And [the] sound of harpists and of musicians and of flute-players and of trumpeters will not be heard in you any more, and every craftsman of every craft will not be found in you any more, and [the] sound of a mill will not be heard in you any more,

23. and [the] light of a lamp will not shine in you any more, and [the] voice of a bridegroom and of a bride will not be heard in you any more, because the merchants of you were the great [ones] of the earth, because by the sorcery of you all the nations were deceived,

24. and in her [the] blood of prophets and of holy [ones] was found, and of all the [ones] having been slain upon the earth.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **18:2** — Sinaiticus reads "prison" (φυλακη) for both unclean spirits and unclean birds. Some manuscripts read "haunt" or "cage" for the second instance. Sinaiticus is consistent with φυλακη in both places.

2. **18:3** — Sinaiticus reads "from the wine of the wrath of the fornication of her" (εκ του οινου του θυμου της πορνειας αυτης), combining wrath and fornication. Some manuscripts omit "wrath" (θυμου). The Sinaiticus reading is preserved literally.

3. **18:5** — "were joined together" (εκολληθησαν) — literally "were glued/cemented unto the heaven." This is a distinctive image of sins accumulated so high they reach heaven.

4. **18:6** — "double the double things" — Sinaiticus reads διπλωσατε τα διπλα, an intensified command. The redundancy is preserved in the literal rendering.

5. **18:8** — Sinaiticus reads "the [one] having judged her" (ο κρινας αυτην), aorist participle, treating the judgment as already accomplished from God's perspective. Some manuscripts read the present participle.

6. **18:14** — Sinaiticus reads "the fruit of the desire of the soul of you" — the genitive chain is preserved literally. The "fruit" (οπωρα) refers specifically to ripe autumn fruit.

7. **18:17** — "everyone sailing upon a place" (πας ο επι τοπον πλεων) — Sinaiticus has this unusual reading. Other manuscripts read "upon the sea" or "to a place." The Sinaiticus reading is preserved as-is.

8. **18:20** — "the God judged the judgment of you from her" — Sinaiticus uses εκρινεν ο θεος το κριμα υμων εξ αυτης, meaning God executed your judgment against her. The cognate accusative (judged the judgment) is preserved literally.

— EMET
